oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Maternal interference and the calf – when it comes to vaccination, what makes sense and what doesn’t
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Chase, Chris
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: calf
immunity
vaccination
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Good calf immunity results from proper maternal care dur­ing gestation, adequate colostral intake and careful neonatal management. Although calves are born with all the essential immune components, their function is suppressed by mater­nal and placental hormones, as well as cortisol surges during birth. Innate immunity is compromised, with reduced comple­ment and interferon production and weak phagocytic ability of neutrophils and macrophages, which improve gradually over the first months of life. Adaptive immunity is more restricted: calves are born without antibodies and depend entirely on co­lostrum for immunoglobulins. B-cell numbers are initially low, delaying antibody production until 3 weeks of age, while T-cell activity is less affected. Maternal antibodies provide early pro­tection, but interfere with vaccine responses, creating a win­dow of susceptibility when antibody levels are insufficient for protection yet high enough to block vaccine efficacy. The timing of vaccination is therefore critical and pathogen-specific. In­tranasal vaccines can bypass maternal interference by priming mucosal immunity, while adjuvanted vaccines, depending on the adjuvant, can be efficacious in the face of maternal anti­body. Management factors – including nutrition, stress reduc­tion, pathogen exposure and biosecurity – further influence immune competence. Effective calf vaccination programs must be tailored to farm-specific risks, maternal antibody decay, and calf development to achieve long-term protection.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 80-86
